metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the RECONCILER_MARKER presence check operates on the ADF-normalized text body (via `_normalize_comment_body` or equivalent `adf_to_text` conversion), NOT on the raw ADF dict field — mirror the pattern in `inbound_differ.py:457` and document this explicitly in the script spec.
evidence: Plan: 'A comment is bridge-bot-authored IFF its raw body contains the reconciler marker'
inbound_differ.py:457: `if RECONCILER_MARKER in body_text:` — checks the TEXT after ADF conversion, not the raw dict
outbound_comments.py:133-137: _normalize_comment_body converts ADF dict via adf_to_text before any string operation
Jira REST returns `body` as ADF dict `{"type": "doc", ...}` — substring check on dict always returns False
findings.finding: The plan specifies the safety-critical bot-author filter as checking whether a comment's 'raw body contains the reconciler marker' — but Jira REST returns comment bodies as ADF dicts, not plain strings, so a direct substring search for RECONCILER_MARKER on the raw body field will fail for every ADF-stored comment. The correct approach (used in inbound_differ.py:457) is to check RECONCILER_MARKER in the ADF-normalized text (after adf_to_text conversion), which is what _normalize_comment_body already does. If implemented literally against the raw ADF dict, the marker filter silently reports no bot-authored comments and the dedup produces an empty delete list — the cleanup does nothing, or worse, if body-type assumptions differ, mislabels comments.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Bot-authored identification (safety-critical filter)' section
scenarios: Implementer checks `RECONCILER_MARKER in comment['body']` where `comment['body']` is an ADF dict — Python evaluates this as 'is the string a key in the dict', which is False for all real comments, silently making the filter pass zero candidates.
Cleanup script produces empty delete-list for every ticket and exits without purging anything, leaving the 13 tickets still capped.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'its raw body contains the reconciler marker' with 'its normalized text body (after _normalize_comment_body / adf_to_text) contains RECONCILER_MARKER — checked on the text output of _normalize_comment_body before the marker-strip step, or equivalently via a pre-strip version of the function'. Note: _normalize_comment_body strips the marker after detecting it is the normalization key; the detection step should use `adf_to_text(body)` or a dedicated predicate that checks the text form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the post-rollback target state explicitly: specify which comments to re-POST on rollback (use the deletion log to identify deleted IDs), assert the expected post-rollback comment count per ticket, and confirm the reconciler will see the correct set of distinct bodies — preventing a restore that returns tickets to the 5000-comment cap.
evidence: 'if a comment must be recovered, re-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
Plan asserts the backup exists and the log artifact exists, but never asserts the JIRA STATE after rollback (comment counts, reconciler-visible state)
ABSENCE: no definition of rollback target state (e.g. 'ticket X should have Y comments, reconciler sees Z distinct bodies') after partial failure

DATA LOSS RISK: re-posting all backed-up comments after partial deletion returns the ticket toward 5000 cap; re-posting only deleted comments requires knowing which IDs were deleted (the deletion log covers this, but the plan does not connect the log to the restore procedure)
findings.finding: The plan describes a rollback path ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but does not assert the TARGET STATE of Jira after a rollback, as required by T11. Specifically: after a partial execution that deleted N comments before aborting, re-POSTing all backed-up comments from those tickets would push them back toward or to the 5000-comment cap — re-creating the very problem being solved. The plan does not define (a) WHICH comments should be re-posted on rollback (all? only the deleted ones?), (b) what the acceptable post-rollback comment count is, or (c) how to determine which backed-up comments are still present vs already deleted, making a safe selective restore operationally undefined.
findings.location: ## What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan / 'Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation)' sub-section; AC: 'Deletion log artifact'
scenarios: Execute run deletes 3000 of 4900 duplicates on REB-155 then hits a fatal error; operator restores from backup by re-POSTing all 5000 backed-up comments, returning the ticket to 5000 cap with mixed old+new timestamps — reconciler now sees a different set of 'oldest' comments than expected and the cap is not lifted
Operator attempts selective restore using the deletion log but the plan gives no procedure for doing so, leaving them with an operationally undefined step under incident pressure
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback procedure section: 'On partial-run abort, use the deletion log (ticket + deleted comment ids) to identify exactly which comments were deleted. Re-POST only those deleted comments (body from backup JSON, matched by id). Expected post-rollback state: comment count = pre-run total minus already-deleted count; if pre-run total was 5000, post-rollback count will be above the point reached mid-run. Verify with GET /comment?maxResults=0 total field. Do NOT re-POST the full backup — that would return the ticket to 5000.' Also add a post-rollback AC: 'After rollback, each affected ticket has comment count = (pre-run total) − (count in deletion log for that ticket), confirmed by metadata call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the deletion log artifact path explicitly (e.g. `artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl`) in both the mechanisms section and AC6, mirroring the backup artifact convention.
evidence: Backup path is explicit: 'artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json'
Log path is absent: no parallel 'artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl' or equivalent is specified.
findings.finding: The deletion log artifact path is never named, unlike the backup artifact which has an explicit path (`artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json`), leaving the executor to invent a location and making AC6's proving command impossible to write without guessing.
findings.location: AC6: 'The deletion log artifact (ticket, comment id, body hash, outcome) exists for the executed run.'
scenarios: AC6's proving command (ls / jq) cannot be written without knowing the path; CI artifact archival cannot be configured.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' checklist and AC6: 'Deletion log written to artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl (one JSON line per deletion attempt: ticket, comment_id, body_hash, outcome).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete test file path (e.g. `tests/test_jira_cleanup.py`) and a `pytest` proving command to ACs 3 and 4 (e.g. `pytest tests/test_jira_cleanup.py -v` exits 0 with all named assertions green).
evidence: AC3: 'A unit test over a synthetic comment set asserts the delete-list the planner computes matches (a)-(d); RED if the filter mislabels a human/unmarked or single-copy comment as deletable.'
AC4: 'a unit test asserts a 429 is retried (not dropped) and Retry-After is honored.'
Neither AC names a test file path (e.g. tests/test_jira_cleanup.py) nor a proving command (e.g. pytest tests/test_jira_cleanup.py -v).
findings.finding: Acceptance criteria 3 and 4 describe unit tests but provide no test file path, no pytest invocation, and no assertion command — leaving no concrete proving command an executor or reviewer can run to confirm the tests exist and are red/green.
findings.location: AC3: 'A unit test over a synthetic comment set asserts the delete-list...'; AC4: 'a unit test asserts a 429 is retried (not dropped) and Retry-After is honored.'
scenarios: A reviewer cannot verify the tests were written; an executor cannot know where to place them; CI cannot target them specifically.
findings.suggested_fix: AC3: append 'Proving command: pytest tests/test_jira_cleanup.py::test_delete_filter_respects_safety_rules -v'. AC4: append 'Proving command: pytest tests/test_jira_cleanup.py::test_delete_retries_on_429 -v'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and a concrete restoration script (or documented shell command using the backup JSON) that re-POSTs comment bodies to each ticket from `backup-<ts>.json`, so the rollback path is executable, not merely described.
evidence: 'if a comment must be recovered, re-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
ABSENCE: No AC line requires a tested or scripted restore procedure; no command is given to bulk re-POST from the backup JSON
T4 rubric: 'acknowledgement alone is insufficient; require the reversibility mechanism'
findings.finding: The rollback path for the ~64k irreversible deletions is described conceptually ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but no concrete restoration script, command, or AC is specified. The T4 criterion requires the REMEDY to be an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism, not merely acknowledgement of the limitation.
findings.location: ## What — Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation) section; Acceptance Criteria (no rollback AC present)
scenarios: A bug in the delete filter mislabels a batch of human comments as bot-authored and deletes them; the operator must manually re-POST hundreds of comments with no tooling
A partial run deletes the wrong 'oldest' copy (e.g. due to a timezone parsing bug in `created`); restoration from backup requires a script that does not exist
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A `--restore` mode (or companion script) reads `backup-<ts>.json` and re-POSTs any comment id listed in the deletion log to its ticket; a dry-run of restore is verified against the backup artifact before any execute run is approved.' Even a documented `jq` + `curl` one-liner referencing the backup schema satisfies this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete `--restore` mode (or a separate restore script) that reads the backup JSON and re-POSTs any comments whose IDs appear in the deletion log, with a dry-run/execute flag and per-comment outcome logging.
evidence: Plan: 'if a comment must be recovered, re-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
No restore script, invocation command, or step-by-step procedure is specified
Backup artifact format is specified (id, body, author, created per ticket) but how to drive a restore from it is not
findings.finding: The rollback/restore procedure for this irreversible operation is described only at a conceptual level ('re-POST its body to the ticket'). No restore script, command, or procedure is specified. For an operation deleting up to ~64,900 comments with no API undo, the restore path must be concrete and executable under pressure.
findings.location: Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation) section
scenarios: A bug in the dedup filter deletes a human comment (despite safety filters); operator needs to restore it urgently from backup but has no script; must write one under pressure
Partial execution deletes comments on 6 of 13 tickets before a crash; operator needs to identify which tickets were processed and restore only those

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, robust
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC6, e.g. `jq -e 'length > 0' artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl` (or equivalent) asserting the log is non-empty and parses, plus a cardinality check that log-line count equals the script-reported deletion count.
evidence: AC1 pairs its existence claim with a concrete proving command: 'python -m json.tool ... >/dev/null + the script's count-reconciliation log line'
AC6 has no parallel proving command — only the prose assertion that the artifact 'exists'.
findings.finding: AC6 ('the deletion log artifact exists') has no proving command — 'exists' is stated without a concrete check (e.g. `ls` + `jq` cardinality assertion), so there is no in-session verifiable evidence of completeness.
findings.location: AC6: 'The deletion log artifact (ticket, comment id, body hash, outcome) exists for the executed run.'
scenarios: A reviewer cannot distinguish 'log file was created but is empty' from a complete log; the cardinality of log lines (should equal total deletions) is never asserted.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6: 'Proving command: python scripts/jira_cleanup_verify.py --log artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl checks len(lines) == script-reported-deletions and each line contains ticket/comment_id/body_hash/outcome fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit 'Write unit tests' work items to the 'What' checklist: one task for the deletion-filter test (AC3: synthetic comment set covering marked/unmarked/single-copy cases) and one for the retry-wrapper test (AC4: mock 429 + Retry-After honored), so both ACs have corresponding planned deliverables.
evidence: AC3: 'A unit test over a synthetic comment set asserts the delete-list the planner computes matches (a)-(d); RED if the filter mislabels a human/unmarked or single-copy comment as deletable.'
AC4: 'a unit test asserts a 429 is retried (not dropped) and Retry-After is honored.'
The 'What' checklist has exactly 5 bullets (Pre-deletion backup, Dry-run default, Keep-one dedup, Operator flow/rejection, Sequencing) — none mention writing or running unit tests.
ABSENCE: no test-implementation task in the work decomposition corresponds to the tests ACs 3 and 4 require.
findings.finding: ACs 3 and 4 each specify a unit test as a named deliverable ('A unit test over a synthetic comment set...' and 'a unit test asserts a 429 is retried...'), but the 'What' work-decomposition checklist contains no corresponding task for implementing these tests. This leaves AC3 and AC4 as orphan criteria — they measure work not planned in the 'What' section — and means implementers have no explicit instruction to write the tests.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, AC4; What (checklist, 5 items); contrast with Concrete mechanisms (rate-limiting, keep-one rule)
scenarios: Implementer follows the 'What' checklist, delivers the script, and considers the plan complete — only to find at review that ACs 3 and 4 are unmet because no tests were written.
The AC3 filter-correctness test is safety-critical (guards against deleting human comments); if it is not planned as explicit work it may be skipped under time pressure.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add two bullets to the 'What' checklist:
- [ ] **Unit test — deletion filter:** write a test over a synthetic comment set that asserts (a) only RECONCILER_MARKER-bearing comments are candidates, (b) oldest per distinct body is kept, (c) single-copy bodies are skipped, (d) unmarked/human comments are never in the delete list. Must be RED if any rule is violated.
- [ ] **Unit test — retry wrapper:** write a test that mocks an HTTP 429 response with a Retry-After header and asserts the wrapper sleeps for at least that duration and retries (not drops) the request.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the pre-deletion backup is (re)taken and completeness-asserted at the START of every `--execute` invocation (not only at dry-run time), and that a stale or absent backup from a prior dry-run does not satisfy the pre-execute gate.
evidence: "Pre-deletion backup: export FULL comment payload...BEFORE any deletion. Proving command: the script asserts, per ticket, len(backup[key]) == jira_reported_total"
"Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation). Jira comment deletion cannot be undone via API. The pre-deletion backup JSON...is the restore artifact"

The plan specifies dry-run -> human review -> execute as separate invocations but never states whether the backup is (re)created at --execute time or only at dry-run time, leaving a potential stale-backup window.
The AC says backup must exist BEFORE any deletion but does not say whether --execute re-validates or re-creates it.
findings.finding: The plan's two-phase operator flow (dry-run in one invocation, --execute in a separate invocation after human approval) does not specify WHEN the pre-deletion backup is taken relative to those two phases. If the backup is taken only during dry-run and --execute is run hours or days later, the backup could be stale (comments may have been added or deleted in the interim), meaning the backup artifact no longer reflects the state at deletion time and the backup completeness assertion could pass against an outdated snapshot. Conversely, if --execute unconditionally re-takes the backup, the plan does not say so, leaving the operator uncertain whether they are protected.
findings.location: ## What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan (inlined) > Pre-deletion backup bullet; AC bullet 1; Concrete mechanisms > Rollback section
scenarios: Operator runs dry-run Monday, reviews diff, approves Tuesday, runs --execute Wednesday. If the backup was taken at dry-run time and not re-taken at execute time, the backup is 2 days stale; a comment posted to one of the 13 tickets in the interim is not in the backup, making content recovery impossible if that comment is erroneously deleted (it cannot be, due to the marker filter — but the backup's completeness assertion would still pass against stale data, giving false confidence).

Operator runs --execute after a partial first execute run. If the backup is re-taken, the total may now differ (some comments deleted), causing the completeness assertion to fail and blocking resumption.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Pre-deletion backup bullet: "The backup is (re)taken at the start of every `--execute` run, immediately before the first DELETE is issued; a backup artifact from a prior dry-run is not accepted as the pre-execute safety gate." Also add a resumption note: on re-run after partial execution, the backup total will be lower than the original; the script should compare against the CURRENT Jira total (not the original) so the completeness check passes correctly on resume.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the backup artifact's sensitivity posture: confirm artifacts/jira-cleanup/ is listed in .gitignore, state the intended retention period and access scope (local-operator-only or controlled CI store), and add a note to delete or archive the file securely after the restore window closes.
evidence: Plan says: 'export FULL comment payload (id, body, author.accountId, author.displayName, created) for all 13 tickets to a versioned JSON artifact under artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json'
Plan says: 're-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore)' — confirming full bodies are in the backup
ABSENCE: no statement on whether artifacts/ is gitignored, whether the CI artifact store is access-controlled, or whether the file should be deleted post-cleanup
findings.finding: The backup artifact (artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json) contains the full comment bodies and author PII (displayName, accountId) of all ~65,000 Jira comments across 13 tickets, but the plan does not declare the artifact's storage posture — specifically whether it may be committed to git, stored as a CI artifact accessible beyond the operator, or treated as ephemeral/local-only.
findings.location: What — T4 safety plan → 'Pre-deletion backup' bullet; Acceptance Criteria → first AC bullet
scenarios: The artifacts/ directory is not in .gitignore; an operator commits the backup as part of the post-run 'versioning'; confidential ticket comment content and user PII is now in the git history.
The file is retained as a CI artifact with broad team read access after the cleanup is complete and the restore window has passed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Artifact sensitivity' note to the safety plan: 'backup-<ts>.json contains full comment bodies and user PII; it MUST NOT be committed to git (add artifacts/jira-cleanup/ to .gitignore if not already present); retain for [N days] post-cleanup then delete securely.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite the Jira Cloud REST API rate-limit documentation (or a measured headroom figure from the existing bot account) to justify ≤10 req/s, or add a short pre-run probe (e.g. 20-DELETE burst on a test ticket) that measures actual 429 threshold before the full run.
evidence: 'Throttle to a conservative default (≤10 req/s, i.e. ≥100ms inter-call sleep)'
'conservative' is an assertion, not derived from a Jira Cloud rate-limit document or a prior measurement of the bot account's headroom
64k+ DELETEs at 10/s ≈ 6400s ≈ 1.75 hours; miscalibration compounds to multi-hour delays
findings.finding: The DELETE throttle rate of ≤10 req/s (100ms inter-call sleep) is asserted as 'conservative' without citing Jira Cloud's published rate-limit ceiling or any measurement. If the actual ceiling is lower, the retry wrapper will fire repeatedly across the ~1.75-hour run, potentially causing a multi-hour stall with no operator signal.
findings.location: ## Concrete mechanisms — Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section
scenarios: Jira Cloud enforces a per-account limit lower than 10 req/s (e.g. 5/s for DELETE specifically); the retry wrapper fires constantly, doubling the wall-clock time with no operator alert
Rate limit is fine at 10/s but the plan gives no expected-duration estimate, so an operator cannot distinguish a 'slow but normal' run from a stuck one
findings.suggested_fix: Add a footnote citing Atlassian's published rate-limit policy for Jira Cloud (currently ~10 req/s per OAuth app for REST v3) and add an AC: 'Script logs cumulative DELETE count and elapsed time every 500 deletes so the operator can confirm expected throughput and abort if stalled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the cleanup script lives inside the `rebar_reconciler` package (preferred, for correct lazy-loader resolution) or as a standalone script with explicit sys.path setup, and document the invocation pattern so `_normalize_comment_body`'s `_load_adf()` lazy-loader resolves `adf.py` from the same directory as in production.
evidence: 'imported and reused verbatim, NOT re-implemented'
outbound_comments.py:50-61: lazy-loader bootstrap that conditionally uses importlib.util.spec_from_file_location when package context is absent
outbound_comments.py:75-85: _load_adf() calls lazy_load(_ADF_KEY, 'adf.py') — requires sibling module resolution
Plan does not state whether the cleanup script is a module inside rebar_reconciler or a standalone script
findings.finding: The plan says to 'import and reuse verbatim' `outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body` in the cleanup script, but `_normalize_comment_body` internally calls `_load_adf()`, which uses a complex lazy-loader bootstrap (tries `from rebar_reconciler._loader import lazy_load`, then falls back to importlib file-path loading). If the cleanup script runs as a standalone script outside the package, this import chain may fail or silently misconfigure. The plan does not specify where the cleanup script lives (inside the package vs. standalone) or how it handles this import dependency.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Dedup key = the reconciler's OWN normalization'
scenarios: Cleanup script is run as `python cleanup_script.py` from an arbitrary directory — import of _normalize_comment_body fails or loads adf.py from wrong path, causing silent misidentification of duplicates
findings.suggested_fix: Place the cleanup script inside `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` (e.g., `jira_cleanup.py`) and invoke it as `python -m rebar_reconciler.jira_cleanup`, ensuring normal package-context import resolves the lazy-loader via `from rebar_reconciler._loader import lazy_load`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit the plan to routing each `delete_comment` call through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (the existing, tested, ADR-0036-compliant path) rather than leaving 'custom wrapper vs. _call_with_retry' as an open implementation choice.
evidence: Plan: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work)... alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'
dispatch_one.py:104-175: _call_with_retry handles urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, honors Retry-After, uses ADR-0036 jitter
acli_graph.py:546-554: delete_comment already calls _direct_rest_delete which raises urllib.error.HTTPError — routing through _call_with_retry requires only wrapping the call site
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_retry.py:98: existing test confirms 429 is retried with Retry-After honored
findings.finding: The plan presents two mutually exclusive retry-routing options for the DELETE loop — 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' OR 'alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry' — without committing to one. `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (lines 104-175) already handles `urllib.error.HTTPError` 429/5xx, honors `Retry-After` per ADR-0036, and is tested across multiple unit tests. The custom-wrapper alternative duplicates existing, validated logic and adds untested code to a safety-critical script. Leaving the choice open invites the implementer to build the inferior (custom) path.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' section
scenarios: Implementer builds a custom retry wrapper that diverges from ADR-0036 jitter, misparses Retry-After, or has a different retry count — introducing subtle rate-limit behavior differences in the most write-heavy script in the codebase.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dual-option text with: 'Route each DELETE through `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` — this reuses the existing 429/5xx + Retry-After + jitter logic (dispatch_one.py:104-175, ADR-0036) and requires no new retry code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default retry count for 429/5xx DELETE failures in the plan (e.g. 'retry up to 5 times with exponential backoff, max sleep 60 s') so the executor needs no judgment call.
evidence: 'retry up to a bounded count; alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'
No numeric bound is given; the executor must invent one (e.g. 3, 5, 10) without guidance.
findings.finding: The retry bound for DELETE failures is described as 'a bounded count' without specifying what that count is, forcing the executor to choose an arbitrary number.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting section: 'retry up to a bounded count'
scenarios: Executor writes a retry loop and must pick a number; different choices produce meaningfully different failure behaviors on a 64k-DELETE run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Retry up to 5 times per DELETE; backoff: min(2^attempt × 1s, 60s); after 5 failures, log the error, skip the comment, and continue (partial-delete is resumable).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-request timeout (e.g. `socket.settimeout` or `urllib.request.urlopen(timeout=...)`) for both the metadata call and the paginated fetch, and surface a clear error with elapsed time if exceeded.
evidence: Plan specifies reuse of `acli.py::get_comments(key)` with `--paginate` but specifies no timeout parameter or timeout wrapper
Plan specifies a dedicated `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` call but no timeout
Retry/backoff is specified only for DELETE 429/5xx, not for the fetch phase
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the paginated `get_comments` fetch or the dedicated `GET .../comment?maxResults=0` metadata call. With 5000 comments × 13 tickets, a slow or hung Jira API will stall the script indefinitely before any writes occur, with no operator-visible signal or recovery path.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination section; AC line 1 (backup completeness check)
scenarios: Jira is slow during the backup phase; script blocks indefinitely on the first of 13 tickets; operator has no visibility into whether it is stuck or just slow
Network partition mid-paginated-fetch leaves the script hung with no timeout to surface the failure
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout=30` (or a configurable value) to every HTTP call in the fetch/backup phase, and catch `socket.timeout`/`urllib.error.URLError` to log a clear message and abort cleanly before any DELETE fires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to verify the accountId single-value assertion: the script must log the resolved bot accountId and abort with a non-zero exit if more than one distinct accountId is found on RECONCILER_MARKER-bearing comments; a unit test or dry-run log line must demonstrate this behavior.
evidence: 'the script asserts that all marked (candidate) comments across the 13 tickets resolve to a single bot accountId and records it — a divergent accountId on a marked comment aborts the run for manual review (defense in depth; the marker still governs)'
ABSENCE: none of AC1–AC6 mention the accountId divergence check, a test for it, or any observable output (logged accountId, abort message) that would allow a reviewer to confirm it is implemented.
findings.finding: The 'Concrete mechanisms' section describes a safety abort: if any RECONCILER_MARKER-bearing comment across the 13 tickets resolves to more than one distinct bot accountId, the run aborts for manual review. This is presented as a 'defense in depth' safety check and is new script-level work, but no AC covers or verifies it, leaving it as uncovered described work.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Bot-authored identification' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria (all 6 ACs)
scenarios: The accountId divergence check is omitted during implementation because it has no AC gating acceptance.
A future bot-account rename silently introduces a second accountId; the unverified abort logic fails to fire, and the safety property degrades without detection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the TOCTOU race in the completeness check: either (a) accept `len(fetched) >= total` (fetched at least as many as the snapshot count), (b) retry the metadata call after the fetch and use the post-fetch total, or (c) document that the script must be run when the 13 tickets are quiesced and explain the abort-and-retry procedure.
evidence: Plan: 'the script reads `total` from that envelope… and reconciles `len(fetched) == total` per ticket, REFUSING to proceed if they differ'
No mention of retry or tolerance for a small delta caused by live activity between the two sequential API calls
13 tickets are capped at 5000 and active enough to have caused the original problem — they are live tickets
findings.finding: The completeness check has a TOCTOU race: `total` is read from a metadata call (`GET .../comment?maxResults=0`) and then compared to `len(fetched)` from a subsequent paginated fetch. On a live Jira instance, a new human comment posted between the two calls makes `len(fetched) > total_at_metadata_time`, causing a spurious mismatch that aborts the run — or worse, if a comment is deleted externally between calls, `len(fetched) < total` and the script refuses to proceed even though the fetch is complete.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination section; AC line 1
scenarios: A human posts a comment between the metadata call and the end of the paginated fetch; `len(fetched)` = 5001 but `total` = 5000; script aborts; operator must re-run and hope the window is quiet
An external process deletes a comment between calls; script refuses to proceed even though backup is complete and correct
findings.suggested_fix: After completing the paginated fetch, re-issue the `GET .../comment?maxResults=0` call to get a post-fetch `total2`, then assert `len(fetched) >= total2` (new comments could have arrived; the backup includes them, which is strictly safer). Record both totals in the log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests asserting the safety-gate failure paths: (a) when `len(fetched) != jira_reported_total`, the script aborts with a non-zero exit and performs zero deletions; (b) when the backup completeness assertion fails, deletion is refused — covering these guards as behavioral tests, not just prose descriptions.
evidence: AC: 'Backup artifact exists and passes its per-ticket completeness check — len(fetched) … equals total … BEFORE any deletion. Proving command: python -m json.tool … + the script's count-reconciliation log line per ticket.'
AC line 3 adds a unit test for the dedup filter and AC line 4 for 429 retry — but no unit test is specified for the pagination/backup assertion failure path (mismatch -> abort).
ABSENCE: no checklist item or AC line specifying a test that injects a mismatched total and asserts the script exits non-zero without deleting anything.
Plan prose: 'REFUSING to proceed if they differ (guards a silently-truncated fetch producing an incomplete/dangerous delete list)' — describes the guard but does not mandate a test for it.
findings.finding: The pagination completeness check (`len(fetched) == total`, guarding against a silently-truncated fetch that would produce an incomplete/dangerous delete list) and the backup completeness assertion (refusing deletion when `len(backup[key]) != jira_reported_total`) are both safety-critical new logic paths, but the AC includes no unit tests asserting these guards actually abort the run on mismatch. For an irreversible bulk-delete operation, the failure path of these safety gates is the most important path to cover — an untested guard that silently passes on a truncated fetch could lead to data loss.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1 (backup completeness) and 'Pagination' section + AC line 1 proving command
scenarios: A bug in the `len(fetched) == total` check (e.g., off-by-one, wrong variable) silently passes, the script proceeds with a truncated comment list, and the delete list omits comments that should be kept — or worse, deletes comments from an incomplete view of the ticket.
The backup assertion silently passes on a file-write failure, and the script proceeds to delete without a valid restore artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two unit tests (can reuse the synthetic comment set already used in AC3): (1) mock `get_comments` to return N-1 items while the metadata call returns N → assert script exits non-zero, 0 DELETEs issued; (2) mock a backup write failure or count mismatch → assert same abort behavior. These tests directly exercise the guards described in the 'Pagination' and 'Pre-deletion backup' sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to routing `delete_comment` calls through the existing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or the re-exported alias in `batch_dispatch`) rather than writing a new 429/5xx retry wrapper; remove 'new script-level work' framing for the retry layer and update the AC unit-test description to test the integration with the existing wrapper, not a bespoke one.
evidence: Plan text: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work)'
Plan text: 'alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'

dispatch_one.py:104-165: _call_with_retry handles JiraAPIError 429/5xx, urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, Retry-After parsing, jittered exponential backoff — identical semantics to what the plan proposes reimplementing
acli_graph.py:546-554: delete_comment already exists and calls _direct_rest_delete — wrapping it in _call_with_retry is a one-line change, not new infrastructure
_call_with_retry is already used at 15+ call-sites in dispatch_one.py, apply_outbound.py, apply_inbound.py, apply_inbound_records.py, batch_dispatch.py — well past Rule-of-Three threshold
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' for the DELETE loop, but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` already implements exactly this — 429+Retry-After honor, 5xx jittered exponential backoff, bounded retry count, and both `JiraAPIError` and `urllib.error.HTTPError` handled (dispatch_one.py:104-165). The alternative ('route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') is mentioned but not the committed path, leaving the default as a reinvention of a tested existing utility.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' paragraph; also AC line 'Rate-limit safety: DELETE loop throttles and retries 429/5xx with backoff; a unit test asserts a 429 is retried'
scenarios: Script author writes a bespoke retry loop that has subtle differences from _call_with_retry (e.g. wrong backoff formula, missing jitter, no Retry-After cap) — divergence grows over time
A unit test written against the bespoke wrapper passes but the shared _call_with_retry behaviour (e.g. ADR-0036 jitter) is not inherited — two retry implementations to maintain
The 'alternatively' clause is treated as optional and the NIH implementation ships by default
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' with 'wrap each `client.delete_comment(key, comment_id)` call in `_call_with_retry` imported from `dispatch_one` (or `batch_dispatch`), which already implements ADR-0036 429/Retry-After + 5xx exponential backoff'. The AC unit test for rate-limit safety can then be a thin integration test verifying the call is routed through `_call_with_retry`, consistent with existing applier tests (e.g. `test_applier_retry.py:98`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'new script-level 429/5xx retry wrapper' with direct use of the existing `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` pattern — the established prior art for every other Jira write — rather than reinventing it; demote the 'alternatively' framing to the primary approach.
evidence: Plan: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work)'
Plan: 'alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'

dispatch_one.py:104-175: `_call_with_retry` handles JiraAPIError 429/5xx, urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, Retry-After header, jittered backoff, max_retries — identical to what the plan proposes reinventing
Every Jira write in the codebase (apply_outbound.py, apply_inbound.py, apply_inbound_records.py, dispatch_one.py) routes through `_call_with_retry` — this IS the prior art
findings.finding: The plan proposes building a new script-level 429/5xx retry wrapper for the DELETE loop, when `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` already provides exactly this — 429+Retry-After, 5xx jittered backoff, max_retries, HTTPError pass-through — and is the established pattern for every other Jira write in the codebase. The plan even acknowledges the alternative ('alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') but demotes it to secondary, defaulting to reinvention.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting + transient-failure handling
scenarios: Script author implements an ad-hoc retry loop that diverges from the tested behavior of `_call_with_retry` (e.g., different Retry-After parsing, different backoff caps, different exhaustion semantics), creating an untested code path for a ~64k DELETE operation.
A future change to the retry policy in `_call_with_retry` is not reflected in the standalone wrapper, causing the cleanup tool to behave differently from the rest of the system.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the rate-limiting mechanism section to: 'Route each DELETE through `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` (the established pattern for all Jira writes in the codebase, dispatch_one.py:104). This inherits 429+Retry-After, 5xx jittered backoff, and max_retries=3 without new code. Add the inter-call sleep (≥100ms) outside the call as a simple `time.sleep(0.1)` between iterations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a probe step (or cite an existing live probe) confirming that `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns a JSON object with a top-level `total` field for the target Jira Cloud instance, before treating this as a reliable completeness oracle.
evidence: Plan: 'GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}; the script reads total from that envelope (new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target)'
search_files for 'maxResults=0' returns no matches in the codebase
No probe-validation note accompanies this assertion, unlike the `delete_comment` method which explicitly says 'Probe-validated: returns 204 on success'
ABSENCE: no code in acli.py, acli_rest.py, acli_cli_ops.py, or acli_graph.py calls the comments endpoint with maxResults=0
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns a `{total, maxResults, startAt, comments:[]}` envelope that can be used to verify fetch completeness, but this specific endpoint+parameter combination has no prior exercise in the codebase and no probe confirmation is cited. The plan labels this 'new script-level work' but does not flag the API behavior as an unverified assumption requiring a probe.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination (fetch ALL 5000, not the first page); AC line 1
scenarios: Jira Cloud returns a different envelope shape (e.g., omits `total` when maxResults=0, or returns an error), causing the completeness check to fail or silently pass with a wrong value, defeating the safety guard before deletions begin.
The endpoint returns total=0 or ignores maxResults=0 in some Jira Cloud versions, causing the backup check to incorrectly refuse to proceed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Probe `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` against a live Jira Cloud instance (e.g. against one of the 13 capped tickets) and confirm the response envelope contains a `total` field with the correct count; record the probe result and actual field name in the plan before implementing the completeness check.
evidence: No codebase matches for `maxResults=0` or GET `/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment` (only DELETE at that path exists in `acli_graph.py:553`)
Plan explicitly labels this 'new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target'

EVIDENCE CLASS: Partially-verified — Jira Cloud REST API v3 documents the comment list GET endpoint with a `total` field in the pagination envelope, but the specific `?maxResults=0` truncation behavior and exact field name are unconfirmed by any probe log or existing repo call
findings.finding: The plan's completeness-check mechanism relies on `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returning a `total` field in the response envelope. This specific endpoint+parameter surface has never been called in the codebase (no repo hits for `maxResults=0` or a direct REST GET of the comment list), and the plan marks it as 'new script-level work'. The behavior — specifically that passing `maxResults=0` returns only the count envelope with a populated `total` field rather than an error or an empty response with no `total` — is a Jira Cloud API contract detail that is Partially-verified (documented but unconfirmed by any probe or existing code in this repo). If the endpoint returns `total` only when `maxResults` is omitted or nonzero, or if the key is named differently (e.g. `total` vs `size`), the pre-deletion safety check silently fails or errors out before any backup is written.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination section — 'obtains the total via a SEPARATE, dedicated metadata call — GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}'
scenarios: If `?maxResults=0` is not supported and returns an error, the completeness check aborts before backup, blocking the entire cleanup.

If the envelope uses a different field name (e.g. `size` in some Jira Server versions), `total` reads as None/missing and the assertion fires a false failure or passes with a 0-count, skipping the safety gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line probe (e.g. `curl -u user:token 'https://<jira>/rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comment?maxResults=0'`) to the plan's spike/pre-work section and capture the actual JSON shape. Alternatively, use `startAt=0&maxResults=1` which is more universally supported and still returns the `total` field in the envelope alongside a minimal `comments` array — confirmed by the same Jira API docs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare and verify the Jira 'Delete Comments' permission posture for the bot service account: state whether it already holds the permission or requires a new scoped grant, specify the minimum project-level scope (not global admin), and add a pre-flight check to the script that validates the permission before any DELETE fires.
evidence: Plan says: 'delete the rest via DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}' — no mention of required Jira project permission
Jira Cloud requires 'Delete Comments' project permission to call that endpoint, separate from 'Add Comments'
acli_rest.py:304 — _direct_rest_delete exists in the codebase but no plan section declares what permission the bot account must hold to invoke it against these 13 projects
ABSENCE: no step in the plan verifies or provisions the Delete Comments permission before execution
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new destructive boundary-crossing path (DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id} to Jira Cloud) but does not declare the access posture or permission scope required for that path. Jira Cloud's 'Delete Comments' project permission is a distinct, broader grant than 'Add Comments'/'Edit Comments' that the bot currently uses. The plan is silent on whether the existing service account already holds it, whether a new grant must be issued, and if so what its project/issue-type scope is — an undeclared access level on a new sensitive destructive path.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section → 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' / 'Keep-one rule' (DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}); Acceptance Criteria → 'Deletion filter provably respects (a)-(d)'
scenarios: The bot service account lacks 'Delete Comments' permission; the --execute run hits 403 Forbidden on the first DELETE, the script aborts mid-run after backup is written but before any cleanup is done — operator must diagnose a permission error under time pressure.
To unblock the script, an operator grants the bot account project-admin or global-admin-level permission, violating least-privilege for a one-time cleanup operation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / permission check' subsection to the plan: (1) confirm via Jira project settings that the bot accountId has 'Delete Comments' on each of the 13 projects (a read-only check the script can perform before the first DELETE); (2) if not already held, document the minimum grant — 'Delete Comments' scoped to the specific projects only, not global; (3) add a --preflight flag that runs this check and exits before the dry-run, so the operator knows the permission situation before committing to the run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step in the dedup grouping rule specifying that `_is_machine_marker_comment(normalized_body)` is applied as a pre-filter to exclude machine-canary comments from the delete-candidate pool entirely (before the keep-one grouping), and extend the AC-3 unit test to assert that a BRIDGE_CANARY_ALERT-prefixed marked comment is never in the delete-list regardless of duplication.
evidence: Plan states: 'Machine-canary comments (_EXCLUDED_COMMENT_PREFIXES: "BRIDGE_CANARY_ALERT:", "Still stale as of") carry volatile timestamps -> each is a distinct normalized body -> each is kept (never a "duplicate" to purge) — correct and safe.'
_is_machine_marker_comment exists at outbound_comments.py:176 and is the production predicate for this exclusion, but is never named in the dedup decision rule.
The plan says to 'Group candidate comments by _normalize_comment_body(body)' with no mention of pre-filtering candidates via _is_machine_marker_comment before grouping.

If any two canary comments happen to produce the same normalized body (e.g. legacy 'Still stale as of' forms with identical content), the keep-one rule would delete one — contrary to the plan's stated intent, because the exclusion predicate was never applied.

The dedup AC (AC line 3) requires a unit test asserting the delete-list but does not name _is_machine_marker_comment as a required test case axis, so the test could pass without exercising machine-marker exclusion.
findings.finding: The plan's canary-comment exclusion protocol is stated only as a consequence of dedup-key uniqueness ('volatile timestamps → each is a distinct normalized body → each is kept') but never specifies applying _is_machine_marker_comment as an explicit pre-filter on the candidate pool — leaving the exclusion rule structurally unco-located from the keep-one grouping rule an executor must implement.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Dedup key = the reconciler's OWN normalization' paragraph; also AC line 3 ('Deletion filter provably respects: (a) marked-only...')
scenarios: An executor implementing the script applies _normalize_comment_body grouping but does not apply _is_machine_marker_comment, relying on the plan's timestamp-uniqueness argument; if a canary comment was re-posted with identical content (e.g. same-second duplicate), the keep-one rule deletes it — an unintended deletion.
The unit test for the deletion filter (AC line 3) is written without a _is_machine_marker_comment test case, so the filter silently fails to guard machine-marker comments that happen to collide.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Dedup key' mechanism paragraph, replace the implicit timestamp-uniqueness argument with an explicit pre-filter step: 'Before grouping, exclude any candidate comment whose normalized body satisfies _is_machine_marker_comment (i.e. starts with _EXCLUDED_COMMENT_PREFIXES) — these are never deletion candidates regardless of duplication count.' Then add a corresponding assertion to the AC-3 unit test.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
